content:
Practical guidance for “Community spare clothing bank” within “Rebuilding · Long-term · Resilience”. It supports clear responsibilities, verified information, visible records, fair priorities, stop conditions, and a workable handover, produces a visible result, and states when to stop or move to a safer fallback.

steps: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Community spare clothing bank” in “Rebuilding · Long-term · Resilience”.

Identify the active hazard, official instruction, safest usable zone, exits, utilities, and people needing assistance. Record the result under “Rebuilding · Long-term · Resilience” before continuing “Community spare clothing bank”.

Inspect from a safe position for smoke, heat, water, gas odour, sparks, falling objects, cracks, unstable surfaces, and blocked routes. Recheck this point before closing “Community spare clothing bank” for “Rebuilding · Long-term · Resilience”.

Move people early to the safer zone or leave when instructed; take medicines, communication, identification, water, and essential mobility aids. Treat any uncertainty here as an unresolved item in “Community spare clothing bank” for “Rebuilding · Long-term · Resilience”.

Control only hazards that can be handled without entering smoke, floodwater, live electricity, gas, fire, or an unstable structure. A second person should be able to verify this part of “Community spare clothing bank”.

Mark unsafe areas, account for everyone, record changes, and reassess after every aftershock, weather shift, alarm, or utility change. Include the outcome of “Community spare clothing bank” in the handover for “Rebuilding · Long-term · Resilience”.
warnings: Do not re-enter a restricted, burning, smoke-filled, flooded, gas-affected, electrically live, or structurally uncertain area. Apply this boundary to “Community spare clothing bank” in “Rebuilding · Long-term · Resilience”.
